PV mRNA differed between diagnoses in RSC and ESC, where SZ subjects had decreased PV in both the RSC ( f SZ vs. CTR: p = 0.037; SZ vs. BP: p = 0.020; and g SZ vs. CTR: p < 0.0001) and ESC ( h SZ vs. CTR: p < 0.0001). PV expression was also decreased in BP subjects compared to CTRs in RSC region 29 ( g p = 0.021) and ESC ( h p = 0.008). A non-significant trend for decreased SST expression was observed in BAd23c, 30, and 29 ( i – k respectively). Each dot represents one subject; data is presented as mean ± standard deviation. *** p < 0.001; ** p < 0.01; * p < 0.05. BP Bipolar Disorder subject group, cc corpus callosum, CTR non-psychiatric control subject group, D dorsal, dPCC dorsal posterior cingulate cortex, ESC ectosplenial cortex, GAD67 glutamic acid decarboxylase 67, M medial, PV parvalbumin, RSC retrosplenial cortex, SST somatostatin, SZ schizophrenia subject group. Diagnosis-specific aMCC gene expression We first determined if aMCC gene expression was different between CTR and the SZ and BP groups. Although the effect of diagnosis was not significant for GAD67 ( F (2,53.255) = 2.145, p = 0.127) and PV ( F (2,54.302) = 2.768, p = 0.072), post-hoc analyses revealed that o.d. measurements were significantly lower versus the CTR group in BA24c’ of BP (Fig. 1g ; p = 0.041) and SZ subjects, respectively (Fig. 1i ; p = 0.028). SZ subjects also showed a trend for decreased PV expression in BA32’ (Fig. 1h ; p = 0.096). For VIP expression, the effect of diagnosis was significant ( F (2,54.150) = 3.420, p = 0.040) with signal intensity lower in SZ subjects across all subregions versus CTRs (Fig. 1j–l ; BA32’; p = 0.036, BA24c’; p = 0.042, BA24ab’; p = 0.044). SST gene expression did not reveal an effect of diagnosis ( F (2,51.632) = 1.329, p = 0.274) or significant post-hoc findings for any subregion (data not shown). For all genes, mixed model ANOVAs showed no significant diagnosis x subregion interactions (data not shown). Diagnosis-specific dPCC/RSC/ESC gene expression Analysis of PV gene expression in dPCC/RSC/ESC showed both a significant effect of diagnosis ( F (2,59.349) = 4.222, p = 0.019) and diagnosis x subregion interaction ( F (10,222.602) = 3.875, p < 0.0001). Subsequent post-hoc analysis indicated that PV gene expression was lower in each of the SZ and BP groups vs. CTR and was confined to the RSC/ESC in both cases (Fig. 2f–h ). In BA30 of the SZ group, PV expression was decreased versus both CTR ( p = 0.037) and BP ( p = 0.020) groups (Fig. 2f ). In contrast, compared to CTR group, PV signal intensity values in BA29 (Fig. 2g ) and BA26 (Fig. 2h ) were each lower in the SZ ( p < 0.0001) and BP groups (BA29, p = 0.021 and BA26, p = 0.008). SST expression was not associated with a significant effect of diagnosis ( F (2,57.725) = 2.122, p = 0.129), but there was an indication of an appreciable trend for reduced gene expression in the dPCC and RSC of BP subjects (Fig. 2i–k ). Compared to the CTR group, this tendency in BP was just above significance (BAd23c; p = 0.091, BA30; p = 0.079, BA29; p = 0.082). There was no effect of diagnosis for VIP ( F (2,55.654) = 0.942, p = 0.396) nor were there diagnosis x subregion interactions (data not shown). Similarly, post-hoc analyses did not reveal any significant gene expression findings for GAD67 and VIP (data not shown). See Fig. 3 for schematic summarizing results. Fig. 3 Summary of interneuron-related gene expression changes. Decreases in the expression of interneuron-related genes (left, GAD67: purple, top to bottom, VIP: yellow, SST: red; PV: green) are summarized for both aMCC (left) and dPCC/RSC/ESC (right) regions. In aMCC, GAD67 expression was reduced in BA24c’ of BP subject vs. CTR. In contrast, aMCC VIP expression was decreased in SZ (32’, 24c’, and 24ab’) compared to CTR, presumably reflecting reduced inhibition of cortical interneurons (e.g., SST and/or PV) as well as decreased inhibition at the apical dendrite of pyramidal neurons. Compared to CTRs, there was a statistical trend for decreased SST expression in BP subjects within the dPCC (regions d23c) and RSC (regions 30 and 29). These potential decreases in SST may occur in Martinotti cells, known to synapse onto the apical tufts of excitatory pyramidal neurons. Decreased PV expression (vs. CTRs) was found in SZ subjects in the aMCC (24c’), RSC (regions 30 and 29) and ESC (region 26), while decreased PV expression in BP subjects (vs. CTRs) was found in the RSC region 29 and ESC (region 26). PV expression is localized to both basket cells and chandelier cells of the aMCC and RSC/ESC, where these neurons contact pyramidal neurons at the soma and initial axon segment, respectively.

[discussion] Discussion The current study was conducted to enhance the understanding of potential GABA-ergic circuit dysfunction in the cingulate gyrus of two disorders, SZ and BP that feature shared neurocognitive impairments of varied magnitude 3 , 4 . Our findings reveal distinct and overlapping transcriptional signatures of schizophrenia (SZ) and bipolar disorder (BP) within specific subregions of Brodmann’s areas in the cingulate gyrus. In this section, we speculate how diagnosis-related dysregulation of specific aMCC GABA-ergic cell-types may impact attention allocation toward action-outcomes and/or affect motor responses associated with the SAN as well as how similar RSC/ESC mechanisms may undermine visuospatial memory and attention distribution in relation to the DMN. Our results highlight both unique and common gene expression patterns that may relate to core symptoms of SZ and BP. Decreased GAD67 and VIP gene expression were exclusive to aMCC, but differentially displayed by BP and SZ subjects, respectively. In contrast, while diminished PV mRNA signal was specific to SZ subjects in aMCC, similar PV deficits were shared by SZ and BP subjects in the RSC/ESC. SST gene expression was not significantly altered in any subregion along the cingulate gyrus extent. However, an appreciable trend for reduced SST mRNA signal was observed in the dPCC/RSC of BP subjects. Here, we discuss the potential neuropsychiatric implications using as a framework previously proposed alterations in dynamic shifting between large-scale neurocognitive networks (DMN, SAN, and CEN) in accordance with the triple network of psychopathology. GAD67 expression Our results showing a non-significant decrease in GAD67 expression within aMCC of SZ subjects are similar to the analysis by Hashimoto et al. 32 . This study also did not find a diagnosis x subregion interaction for GAD67 mRNA levels, which were unchanged intrinsically, but rather comparably decreased when statistically considered along with expression within visuomotor network nodes (DLPFC, primary motor, and visual cortices). In contrast, some aMCC studies evaluating potential changes in SZ have reported a general non-subregional reduction in GAD67 mRNA 33 , 34 as well as diminished GABA 34 . Taken together with the current analysis, it remains debatable how well local GAD67 mRNA expression aligns with the reduced basal aMCC GABA levels described by SZ meta-analyses 23 , 24 . Our results also put into question the potential and somewhat controversial association of reported cortical interneuron loss with GAD67 mRNA deficits in SZ. For example, in the adjacent pACC, decreased GAD67 mRNA expressing neurons 35 as well as fewer non-pyramidal neurons, presumably interneurons, particularly in layers II and V 28 – 30 , have been shown. Conversely, DLPFC analyses suggest that interneurons may be viable but express undetectable mRNA levels for GAD67 36 , SST (layer II) 37 , and PV (layer IV) 37 , without cell loss. In the case of aMCC, SZ subjects have been shown to exhibit a trend for blunted density of BA24c’ layer II calbindin-positive interneurons 38 , no layer change in PV density 38 , and increased layer V/VI non-subtype specific neurons 39 . Future research in aMCC layer II could further explore whether the loss of GABAergic cells is linked to SZ. Our aMCC findings in BP subjects reveal reduced BA24c’ GAD67 mRNA. Previous neuroimaging studies examining BP patients collectively suggest no difference in GABA within that general region 40 . In addition, Ramaker et al. 34 , despite sampling aMCC in an overlapping subject cohort with the current investigation, did not observe reduced GAD67 mRNA in BP. However, the same study did uncover reduced GABA which parallels our GAD67 results. While the current findings oppose a report of an overall increase in aMCC BA24c’ neuronal density within layer VI of BP subjects 39 , they are more consistent with pACC data in BP showing a consistent reduction of layer II non-pyramidal neuron density 28 – 30 , decreased GAD67 mRNA-positive neurons 35 , and fewer GAD65-positive terminals 41 . From a functional standpoint, altered aMCC GABA-ergic systems in BP would be expected to impact encoding evaluative feedback from conflict-driven action-outcomes for future decision-making 42 , 43 . Normal aMCC activity elicited by incorrect choices during this process generates a GABA-mediated 44 error-related negativity (ERN) measure 45 , 46 that is attenuated across patients with SZ 47 – 49 , psychotic-bipolar 47 , 49 , and euthymic-bipolar when controlling for depressive symptoms 50 . A diminished ERN is also connected to poor real-world functioning, negative symptom severity, and compromised executive function in psychotic disorders 49 . Such manifestations may be linked to aberrant large-scale neurocognitive network interactions, particularly concerning the basal ganglia. Meta-analysis demonstrates a strikingly common reduction in aMCC-caudate FC across first-episode psychosis (schizophrenia and schizoaffective disorder), BP, and psychotic-bipolar patients 51 . Considering the caudate also functions as a SAN node, aMCC GAD67 mRNA deficits in BP subjects may contribute to psychomotor deficits through aberrant corticostriatal circuits and blunted error processing/action monitoring, thereby supporting the triple network hypothesis of psychopathology. Parvalbumin expression The neurocognitive implications of decreased PV gene expression in aMCC (BA24c’) and RSC/ESC (BA30/29/26) of SZ as well as BP (BA29/26) subjects can be posited from the neurophysiological properties of PV cells along with neuroanatomical and functional connectivity. Most PV interneurons are fast-spiking basket cells which harness perisomal synapses for feedforward and feedback inhibition and the generation of gamma band oscillations for synchronizing activity of pyramidal cell assemblies 31 , 52 . Convincing arguments have been made for connecting reduced DLPFC PV gene expression and GABA-ergic inhibition of local pyramidal cells to altered gamma oscillatory activity and cognitive deficits in SZ 52 . The current results combined with other work in SZ revealing a trend toward decreased aMCC PV mRNA 32 alludes to potentially similar deleterious effects of dysregulated BA24c’ gamma oscillatory activity on cognition. Several SZ studies examining PV interneurons in the BA24c region of pACC indicate reduced somal size and dendritic tree extent as well as increased axonal cartridges and layer V PV+ cell count 53 – 55 . Although the latter concurs with heightened aMCC layer V/VI neuronal density in SZ 39 , our results imply an overall hypofunction of BA24c’ PV interneurons, potentially affecting local gamma band oscillatory activity. Functional imaging studies often show that tasks associated with cognitive motor control coincides with BA32’/24c’ co-activation, thus acquiring the collective term dorsal aMCC (daMCC) (see review by Vogt et al.) 8 . This collaboration is of particular importance considering our results demonstrate that SZ subjects also displayed a trend for reduced PV expression in BA32’. The functional capacity of daMCC is expansive with its activity implicated in divided attention, movement anticipation, and action initiation while also serving as the cingulate region engaged in the monitoring of ongoing action-outcomes 8 and producing ERN responses 56 . Upper primate studies indicate BA24c’ function is mediated by reciprocal connections with DLPFC and premotor/supplementary motor cortex in concert with cingulospinal projections 57 – 60 . Given that DLPFC exhibits particularly strong FC with daMCC 45 , 61 and classifies as both a SAN node and CEN hub 20 , it is tempting to hypothesize that decreased daMCC PV expression may point to desynchronization of aMCC-DLPFC gamma oscillatory activity during cognitive motor control behaviors in SZ patients. In support, FC between BA24c’ and DLPFC is reduced in SZ subjects during verbal fluency tasks 62 . Within the caudal cingulate gyrus, RSC/ESC displayed the highest PV gene expression in controls while exhibiting reductions in SZ (BA30/29/26) and BP (29/26). Importantly, interpretating these results within context of previous RSC analyses is made challenging due to potential confounds regarding past neuroanatomical atlases/schematics that often incorrectly place its location caudal to the corpus callosum splenium on the surface of the caudomedial lobule, which is actually ventral PCC as discussed by Vogt et al. 63 . Our in situ hybridization assessment was conducted dorsal to the splenium and adjacent to the posterior MCC within what we refer to as anterior RSC. Careful attention to brain coordinates reveals anterior RSC likely shares functional similarities with its caudal counterpart where lesions induce impairment of visuospatial memory, impaired navigation, and episodic memory 11 . Indeed, anterior RSC activation couples with location/action recollection 10 , 64 , anticipatory allocation of spatial attention 65 , autobiographical memory 18 , and memory encoding 66 . In contrast, while caudal-ventral RSC is an integrated node of the self-referential DMN known to be dysregulated in SZ and BP, the role of anterior RSC is less clear. Our data demonstrates trends toward lower PV mRNA values in RSC (BA30/29))/ESC (BA26) in SZ versus BP, reaching significance in BA30. These findings are conceptually in-line with overlapping neurocognitive deficits in SZ and BP where SZ patients display greater impairment in attention/speed of processing and visual memory 3 , 4 . Compared to controls, SZ patients demonstrate increased anterior RSC activity during self-reflection 67 , which is influenced by visuospatial attention 68 , as well as enhanced FC with the lingual gyrus 69 , indicating heightened interaction with visual systems. SZ patients experiencing auditory verbal hallucinations exhibit increased anterior RSC-DMN FC and decreased anterior RSC-hippocampal formation FC 70 – 72 . In addition, less anterior RSC deactivation is observed during verbal fluency tasks in both SZ and BP patients 73 . Collectively, lower anterior RSC PV gene expression points to the possibility of altered gamma oscillations contributing to both positive and negative symptoms, particularly in SZ. To our knowledge, the current study is among the initial quantitative analyses of gene expression performed in ESC (BA26). Positioned lateral to anterior RSC BA29 and dorsal to the hippocampal rudiment, also referred to as indusium griseum 63 , downregulation of BA26 PV gene expression in SZ and BP is more difficult to interpret. Precise ESC function is unknown due to, in part, its diminutive size being less than ideal for functional imaging assessments 63 . Adjacent to the uniformly reduced PV gene expression in BA30/29, we hypothesize ESC in SZ and BP subjects may exhibit disorganized filtering of unrefined visuospatial memory-related information. The latter is likely to be communicated to a compromised anterior RSC in SZ and BP for further processing via ESC-BA29 interconnections. Somatostatin expression Previous cortical studies focused on SZ and BP have shown SST mRNA to be one of the more downregulated of the major interneuron markers with respect to magnitude of change 32 , 74 , 75 . Interestingly, the current cingulate analyses did not uncover significant findings amongst any cingulate subregion. Instead, we report non-significant decreases in subregional aMCC SST gene expression in SZ. This aligns more closely with findings of decreased SST mRNA demonstrated by Hashimoto et al. 32 , which did not show a diagnosis x region interaction but rather demonstrated an overall reduction in SST when analyzed in conjunction with other nodes in the long-range visuomotor network. On the other hand, there were remarkably stronger trends, just missing significance, for decreased SST expression in dPCC (BAd23c) and RSC (BA30/29) of BP subjects that mirror the PV reductions seen ventrally in both SZ and BP. Furthermore, our findings agree with prior investigations of orbital frontal cortex and DLPFC showing comparatively lower mean SST mRNA levels in BP versus SZ 74 . Consequently, the inherently different synaptic patterns of SST+ interneurons upon pyramidal cells versus PV+ cells dictates that dPCC/RSC/ESC information processing may to differ between SZ and BP thereby indicating one potential link to unique yet overlapping clinical features. SST+ cells are largely Martinotti type and synapse on distal dendrites of pyramidal cells 31 which receive afferent input from thalamic, dopaminergic, and long-range cortical afferents 76 . Thus, a tendency for decreased dPCC/RSC SST expression in BP subjects may reflect an enhanced liability for dysregulated sharpening of input from long-range cortical and subcortical afferents. Indeed, SST+ neurons are important for synchronizing the activities of distant brain regions to achieve optimal long-range cortico-cortical transmissions 77 , 78 . More specifically, while SST interneurons have been linked to generation of theta oscillations 79 , animal models demonstrate that disruption of SST+ cells may also minimize gamma oscillatory power 80 , 81 . Thus, potential alterations in gamma and/or theta band oscillatory activity, reflected by a trend for reduced SST mRNA, may be associated with disrupted local circuits as well as large-scale cognitive networks that contribute to compromised attention set shifting and visuospatial memory in BP. Vasoactive intestinal peptide expression Significant decreases in VIP gene expression were confined to aMCC of SZ subjects and present across all subregions (BA32’/24c’/24ab’). It is noteworthy to mention that decreased VIP expression in BA24ab’ of SZ subjects constitutes the only altered transcript in this region irrespective of diagnosis. Few cortical studies have investigated cortical VIP gene expression in psychiatric illness. Of these, reduced transcription has been reported in DLPFC and orbital frontal cortex of both SZ and BP 74 as well as the primary visual cortex in SZ subjects 75 . VIP + GABA-ergic interneurons receive long-range cortical afferent input and specialize in disinhibitory processes by primarily innervating pyramidal dendrite-targeting SST+ cells thereby increasing the gain of excitatory pyramidal neurons 31 . Altered gain control mediated by BA32’/24c’ (daMCC) VIP+ interneurons in SZ may contribute to impaired action monitoring, especially that related to processing of visuomotor incongruence 82 . Alternatively, similar dysregulation in BA24ab’ of SZ subjects may be of particular importance regarding avoidance/escape behaviors driven by daMCC 7 , 8 considering BA24ab’ receives greater input from the amygdala 57 and is uniquely activated by fear 8 . Rodent studies show that deactivation of VIP+ interneurons in ACC or mPFC is associated with more time spent in open arms during elevated maze testing 83 , 84 . In Humans, neuroimaging assessments reveal that BA24ab’ exhibits decreased activity in SZ versus control subjects at rest 85 as well as hypodeactivation during a facial emotion discrimination requiring matching/labeling of fear and anger depictions 86 . Similar studies show patients diagnosed as having high negative schizotypy with anhedonia demonstrate reduced FC between BA24ab’ and amygdala under both fear and happy conditions 69 . Furthermore, decreased FC between BA24ab’ and putamen is positively and negatively associated with delusion score and antipsychotic treatment, respectively 87 . Therefore, decreased BA24ab’ VIP expression in SZ subjects revealed here may point to the dysregulation of pyramidal cell gain control required for current and future avoidance behaviors related to fear in SZ patients. Limitations A limitation of the current study involves analyzing cryosections, without corresponding Nissl staining, from archived cingulate blocks extracted from frozen coronal brain slabs. Variable dissection outcomes due to multiple dissectors led to some subjects not having available, fully or partially, various targeted BAs. However, location of retained BA subregions could be approximated (see Figs. 1 a and 2a ) by comparing the positions of cell-based boundaries, as defined by previously published cingulate maps 5 , 6 , to visual interrelationships between anatomical landmarks and observed dorsoventral PV mRNA gradients which are in excellent agreement with immunohistochemical patterns 88 . Given the total area (mm 2 ) variability of BAs available for quantification, e.g., due to artifacts and/or or gross dissection differences, we acquired a single optical density value representing the average signal intensity above background for each BA. This measurement, corresponding to the entire available area within an outlined BA, differs from previous cortical studies that often sample smaller but consistent representative areas for acquiring cell counts to measure neuronal density. Consequently, an important question as to whether reduced aMCC gene expression may relate to lower interneuron density/loss, as indicated by some SZ and BP studies in pACC 28 – 30 , cannot be gleaned from the current study. In addition, without neurohistological identification of precise cortical layer borders, quantified BA subregions may have included minor contributions from superficial interstitial white matter just below cortical layer VI. Quantitative implications of the latter, if any, are unknown. However, it is notable that SZ subjects have been shown to display increased interneuron density in DLPFC superficial white matter as measured by GAD65/67 protein 89 and SST mRNA 90 . Similarly, both SZ and BP subjects demonstrate a 2-fold increase in density of white matter-localized NeuN+ neurons in aMCC 91 . Nevertheless, the present study still highlights diagnosis-related gene expression differences within functionally diverse cingulate subregions that should be considered BA-enriched.

[conclusion] Conclusions In summary, the present study uncovered divergent and overlapping reductions in interneuron subtype-specific gene expression within the rostral and caudal cingulate gyrus of SZ and BP subjects. In aMCC, altered PV and VIP mRNA signals were exclusive to SZ subjects and raise the possibility more prominent interneuron-related impairment versus BP that may lend to altered error/action monitoring and psychomotor behavior. Caudally, decreased PV gene expression was exhibited by both SZ and BP subjects in the RSC/ESC, which may reflect shared circuits affecting visuospatial memory processing and recollection. These results show unique diagnosis-related gene expression patterns in the cingulate gyrus of SZ and BP subjects and highlight neuroanatomical targets for conducting future psychiatric studies.

retrieved_papers.title: Increased platelet intracellular calcium ion concentration is specific to bipolar disorder.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2014]
[Abstract] Increased baseline ([Ca(2+)]B) and agonist-stimulated ([Ca(2+)]s) free intracellular calcium ion concentrations ([Ca(2+)]i) are well-replicated findings in bipolar disorder, but whether this finding is specific to that condition and if so, whether it is a marker of the mood disorder or a feature seen in other disorders such as psychosis has remained unclear.
Platelet [Ca(2+)]i was assessed in 15 inpatients with psychotic and nonpsychotic mania, 17 schizophrenia inpatients, and 17 matched controls.
Platelet [Ca(2+)]B and [Ca(2+)]s were significantly higher than controls in bipolar disorder but not schizophrenia. Variability of [Ca(2+)]B was significantly increased in bipolar disorder regardless of the presence of psychosis, but not in schizophrenia.
Use of antipsychotic drugs by the majority of both patient groups may have obscured elevated [Ca(2+)]i in schizophrenia, or may have masked a difference between psychotic and nonpsychotic bipolar disorder. Measurement of [Ca(2+)]i is too labor intensive to become a routine test for diagnosis or prediction of treatment response.
Elevated intracellular Ca(2+) signaling may be a marker of primary cellular hyperactivity that could contribute to comorbid conditions such as hypertension and neuronal apoptosis. Since lithium and carbamazepine attenuate increased [Ca(2+)]i, further research may demonstrate a correlation between normalization of [Ca(2+)]i and response to one of these medications, and further research may clarify whether a subgroup of patients may respond well to calcium channel antagonists.

[conclusion] Conclusions Mixed results of studies of cellular actions of valproate on intracellular calcium signaling may be the results of use of subjects with milder illness and normal baseline signaling. Studies of animals and normal human subjects may be less informative about the potential mechanism of action of any psychotropic medication than studies of patients with sufficient severity of illness and abnormalities in the dimension of cellular function targeted by the medication.

retrieved_papers.title: Disrupted-in-schizophrenia1 (DISC1) L100P mutation alters synaptic transmission and plasticity in the hippocampus and causes recognition memory deficits.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2016]
[Abstract] Disrupted-in-schizophrenia 1(DISC1) is a promising candidate susceptibility gene for a spectrum of psychiatric illnesses that share cognitive impairments in common, including schizophrenia, bipolar disorder and major depression. Here we report that DISC1 L100P homozygous mutant shows normal anxiety- and depression-like behavior, but impaired object recognition which is prevented by administration of atypical antipsychotic drug clozapine. Ca2+ image analysis reveals suppression of glutamate-evoked elevation of cytoplasmic [Ca2+] in L100P hippocampal slices. L100P mutant slices exhibit decreased excitatory synaptic transmission (sEPSCs and mEPSCs) in dentate gyrus (DG) and impaired long-term potentiation in the CA1 region of the hippocampus. L100P mutation does not alter proteins expression of the excitatory synaptic markers, PSD95 and synapsin-1; neither does it changes dendrites morphology of primary cultured hippocampal neurons. Our findings suggest that the existence of abnormal synaptic transmission and plasticity in hippocampal network may disrupt declarative information processing and contribute to recognition deficits in DISC1 L100P mutant mice.

[Introduction] Introduction Major neuropsychiatric illnesses such as schizophrenia, bipolar disorder, major depression and autism spectrum disorder are genetically complex but share not only overlapping symptoms (for example, cognition deficits in learning, memory and attention) and environmental risk factors (for example, influenza, trauma and stress), but also molecular etiology [ 1 ]. In particular, disrupted-in-schizophrenia 1 ( DISC1 ) gene has been identified as one of the putative susceptibility genes to schizophrenia and other major mental illnesses in multiple pedigrees [ 2 ]. DISC1 was originally discovered in a large Scottish pedigree showing a heavy burden of major psychiatric disorders associated with balanced chromosomal translocation (1:11)(q42.1:q14.3) [ 3 ]. There is broad agreement that studying rare, highly penetrant risk mutations, for example DISC1 , in animal models can not only shed light on the neural integrity of DISC1 and its relevance to neuropsychiatric disorders, but also help to decipher gene-environment interactions in those illnesses. Quite a few animal models with DISC1 mutations have reported altered brain morphology, abnormal plasticity, cognitive and affective deficits [ 4 – 9 ]. N -ethyl- N -nitrosourea (ENU)-induced inheritable missense point mutations in exon 2 of the mouse DISC1 gene have been of particular interest since Q31L mutant mice showed depression-like behaviors while L100P mutants showed schizophrenia-like phenotypes [ 4 ]. DISC1 L100P and Q31L mutant mice have been considered as potential animal models of psychiatric disorders [ 10 – 12 ]. However, other independent research groups recently reported that no typical schizophrenia-like or depression-like behaviors were observed in DISC1 L100P or Q31L mutants respectively [ 13 ]. This discrepancy may be explained by differences in the genetic background of the mice, the laboratory environment, experimental design and more. A very recent work shows that ENU-generated DISC1 L100P mice have multiple ENU-induced mutations which sum up to produce the phenotype [ 14 ]. Nevertheless, it suggests that further studies are needed to elucidate the cause of behavioral variance associated with DISC1 L100P or Q31L mutants and to determine whether these mice strains are suitable animal models to study schizophrenia and depression. In this study, we further analyzed the cognitive and affective behaviors of L100P homozygotes and their WT littermates, and explored the underlying synaptic mechanisms as well. We found that L100P mice show normal affective behaviors but obvious deficits in object recognition which is prevented by clozapine, but not haloperidol. Dentate gyrus (DG) granule cells carrying homozygous mutation of L100P exhibit decreased synaptic excitation and intact synaptic inhibition, meanwhile Schaffer collateral-CA1 synapses of L100P mice display impaired synaptic plasticity. Supportively, both CA1 region and DG of L100P mice hippocampus shows suppression of intracellular [Ca 2+ ] elevation after glutamate challenge. However, L100P mutation does not affect expression of the excitatory synaptic markers (PSD-95 and synapsin-1) or dendrites morphology of hippocampal neurons. Our findings thus suggest that the existence of altered synaptic transmission and plasticity may disrupt declarative information processing in the hippocampal network and contribute to recognition deficits observed in DISC1 L100P mutant mice. Rather normal dendritic structure and excitatory synaptogenesis may correlate to normal affective behaviors observed in L100P mutant mice.
